question: [File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: wExp / tickToPrice] Can an unprivileged lender, by taking a buy offer at a tick where the 3rd-order Taylor series in wExp systematically underestimates the true exponential, acquire credit units at a price lower than the mathematically correct price, so that at maturity withdraw() redeems those units 1:1 for more loan tokens than were economically paid? Preconditions: market created, lender places buy offer at tick t where wExp approximation error is maximal (~1.4 bps per test/TickLibTest.sol:133); Attacker action: taker fills the buy offer for large units; Call sequence: take(offer, ..., units, ...) -> withdraw(market, units, lender, receiver) post-maturity; Invariant tested: sum(buyerAssets paid across all takes) >= sum(units redeemed * 1 loan token); Scoped impact: lender redeems more loan tokens than deposited; Proof idea: fuzz tick in [1400,4600] where wExp error is largest, assert buyerAssets * WAD / units < tickToPrice(tick) - epsilon, then simulate maturity redemption.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP=1e12 floor-truncation in tickToPrice (line 50: .divHalfDownUnchecked(PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP) * PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP) to systematically acquire credit units at a price up to 1e12-1 WAD units below the true mathematical price, enabling over-redemption at maturity? Preconditions: market with any valid tick spacing; Attacker action: lender places buy offer at tick t where tickToPrice(t) is just above a PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP boundary; Call sequence: take(buyOffer, ..., units, ...) -> warp(maturity+1) -> withdraw(market, units, lender, receiver); Invariant tested: tickToPrice(t) * units / WAD <= actual loan tokens redeemable; Scoped impact: lender redeems up to units * 1e12 / WAD extra loan tokens per take; Proof idea: for all ticks 0..MAX_TICK assert tickToPrice(t) <= exact_price(t) and measure cumulative shortfall across large units.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: divHalfDownUnchecked] Can an unprivileged actor trigger an arithmetic overflow in divHalfDownUnchecked (line 19: unchecked { return (x + (d - 1) / 2) / d; }) when x is near type(uint256).max and d is 2, causing the intermediate sum x + (d-1)/2 to wrap to a small value and return a price far below the true value, so that credit units are issued at near-zero cost? Preconditions: tickToPrice called with a tick whose wExp output causes 1e36 / (1e18 + wExp(...)) to be near type(uint256).max; Attacker action: craft offer at such a tick; Call sequence: take(offer, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: divHalfDownUnchecked(x, d) == floor((x + floor((d-1)/2)) / d) without overflow; Scoped impact: credit units issued at zero or near-zero cost, redeemable 1:1 at maturity; Proof idea: unit test divHalfDownUnchecked with x = type(uint256).max - 1, d = 2 and assert result equals expected.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: wExp (unchecked block)] Can an unprivileged actor trigger an overflow in the unchecked wExp computation (line 33: int256 secondTerm = r * r / (2 * 1e18); line 34: int256 thirdTerm = secondTerm * r / (3 * 1e18)) for inputs near the maximum valid range, causing expR to wrap to a negative or unexpectedly small value, which after uint256 cast and left-shift produces a wildly incorrect price? Preconditions: wExp called with x near LN_ONE_PLUS_DELTA * MAX_TICK/2 (maximum positive input from tickToPrice); Attacker action: place offer at tick 0 (maximum wExp input); Call sequence: take(offer, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: wExp(x) > 0 for all valid inputs; Scoped impact: tickToPrice returns incorrect price, credit units issued at wrong cost; Proof idea: fuzz int256 x in [0, LN_ONE_PLUS_DELTA * MAX_TICK/2] and assert wExp(x) > 0 and wExp(x) < type(uint256).max.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: wExp (bit shift)] Can an unprivileged actor cause an overflow in the left-shift operation (line 39: return uint256(expR) << uint256(q)) in wExp when q is large, producing a wrapped-around price that is far below the true value, so that credit units are acquired at near-zero cost? Preconditions: wExp called with large positive x; q = (x + offset) / ln2 could be large enough that uint256(expR) << q overflows; Attacker action: place buy offer at tick 0 (largest wExp input); Call sequence: take(buyOffer, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: uint256(expR) << uint256(q) does not overflow for all valid tick inputs; Scoped impact: tickToPrice returns 0 or tiny value, buyer acquires credit units for free; Proof idea: compute max q for tick=0 input and verify uint256(expR) << q < type(uint256).max.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: wExp (negative branch)] Can an unprivileged actor exploit precision loss in the negative branch of wExp (line 26: return 1e36 / wExp(-x)) where wExp(-x) is very large, causing the division to truncate to 0 or 1, so that tickToPrice returns 0 for ticks near MAX_TICK, enabling a buyer to acquire credit units for 0 assets? Preconditions: tick near MAX_TICK where LN_ONE_PLUS_DELTA * (MAX_TICK/2 - tick) is large and negative; Attacker action: place buy offer at tick near MAX_TICK; Call sequence: take(buyOffer, ..., units, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: tickToPrice(t) > 0 for all t in [1, MAX_TICK]; Scoped impact: buyer acquires credit units for 0 loan tokens, redeems 1:1 at maturity; Proof idea: assert tickToPrice(t) > 0 for all t in [1, MAX_TICK] and verify 1e36 / wExp(-x) > 0 for all valid negative x inputs.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the fact that tickToPrice(0) = 0 and tickToPrice(1) = 0 (first non-zero price is at tick 2 = 1e12) to place a buy offer at tick 0 or tick 1 and acquire credit units for 0 buyerAssets (since units.mulDivDown(0, WAD) = 0), then redeem those units 1:1 at maturity? Preconditions: market with tickSpacing=1 (after spacing reduction) or DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=4 (tick 0 and 4 are multiples); Attacker action: lender places buy offer at tick 0; Call sequence: take(buyOffer at tick 0, ..., units, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: buyerAssets > 0 whenever units > 0 and credit increases; Scoped impact: lender acquires credit units for free, redeems loan tokens at maturity; Proof idea: assert take with tick=0 and units>0 results in buyerAssets=0 and creditIncrease>0.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (mulDivDown rounding)] Can an unprivileged lender, by taking a buy offer with units=1 at any tick where tickToPrice(tick) < WAD, cause buyerAssets = units.mulDivDown(buyerPrice, WAD) = 0 (since 1 * buyerPrice / WAD rounds to 0 for buyerPrice < WAD), acquiring 1 credit unit for 0 loan tokens, redeemable 1:1 at maturity? Preconditions: market with any tick < MAX_TICK; Attacker action: lender takes buy offer with units=1; Call sequence: take(buyOffer, ..., 1, ...) -> withdraw(market, 1, lender, receiver) post-maturity; Invariant tested: buyerAssets >= 1 whenever buyerCreditIncrease >= 1; Scoped impact: lender acquires credit unit for 0 cost, redeems 1 loan token at maturity; Proof idea: assert for all ticks t < MAX_TICK that take with units=1 results in buyerAssets >= 1.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: settlementFee / tickToPrice interaction in take] Can an unprivileged lender place a buy offer at the lowest tick where tickToPrice(tick) > 0 (tick=2, price=1e12) and set settlementFee to its maximum at TTM=0 (MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_0_DAYS = 0.000014e18 = 14e12), causing sellerPrice = offerPrice - settlementFee to underflow and revert, or alternatively at a tick where offerPrice is just above settlementFee so sellerPrice rounds to 0 and sellerAssets=0 while buyerAssets>0, creating a free credit acquisition? Preconditions: market at maturity (TTM=0), settlementFeeCbp0 set to max; Attacker action: lender places buy offer at tick where tickToPrice(tick) is just above settlementFee; Call sequence: take(buyOffer, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: sellerAssets > 0 whenever buyerCreditIncrease > 0 and units > 0; Scoped impact: lender acquires credit units with sellerAssets=0, redeems 1:1 at maturity; Proof idea: fuzz tick and settlementFee, assert sellerAssets > 0 when buyerCreditIncrease > 0.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: settlementFee (interpolation truncation) / take] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the integer division truncation in settlementFee (line 979: (feeLower * (end - timeToMaturity) + feeUpper * (timeToMaturity - start)) / (end - start)) to obtain a settlement fee that is up to (end - start - 1) / (end - start) less than the mathematical value, causing buyerPrice to be understated and credit units to be acquired below true economic cost? Preconditions: market with non-zero settlement fee breakpoints, TTM between two breakpoints; Attacker action: lender takes buy offer at TTM where interpolation truncation is maximal; Call sequence: take(buyOffer, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: actual settlement fee >= floor(mathematical interpolation); Scoped impact: lender pays fewer assets per credit unit than economically correct; Proof idea: for all TTM values in each breakpoint interval, assert settlementFee(id, ttm) >= floor(exact_interpolation(ttm)).

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: priceToTick / tickToPrice (round-trip)] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the priceToTick round-up-to-spacing behavior (line 67: (low + spacing - 1) / spacing * spacing) to obtain a tick whose tickToPrice is strictly greater than the input price, then place a buy offer at that tick and acquire credit units at a price higher than intended by the maker, causing the maker (seller) to receive fewer assets than expected while the buyer redeems 1:1 at maturity? Preconditions: market with spacing > 1; Attacker action: lender uses priceToTick to find tick, places buy offer at that tick; Call sequence: priceToTick(targetPrice, spacing) -> take(buyOffer at returned tick, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: tickToPrice(priceToTick(p, s)) >= p for all valid p, s; Scoped impact: maker (seller) receives fewer assets than expected; Proof idea: fuzz price and spacing, assert tickToPrice(priceToTick(price, spacing)) >= price.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: priceToTick (spacing rounding beyond MAX_TICK)] Can an unprivileged market creator or taker trigger priceToTick to return a tick greater than MAX_TICK when the binary search finds low=MAX_TICK and spacing rounding (line 67: (low + spacing - 1) / spacing * spacing) pushes the result above MAX_TICK, causing a subsequent tickToPrice call to revert with TickOutOfRange, or alternatively causing an offer to be placed at an inaccessible tick? Preconditions: priceToTick called with price=0 and spacing > 1 where MAX_TICK % spacing != 0; Attacker action: market creator sets spacing that does not divide MAX_TICK; Call sequence: priceToTick(0, spacing) -> take(offer at returned tick, ...); Invariant tested: priceToTick(price, spacing) <= MAX_TICK for all valid inputs; Scoped impact: DoS of take or incorrect tick used for offer pricing; Proof idea: fuzz spacing values that do not divide MAX_TICK=5820 and assert priceToTick(0, spacing) <= MAX_TICK.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: wExp (monotonicity at q-boundary)] Can an unprivileged lender exploit a non-monotonicity in wExp at the boundary between consecutive q values (where 2^q * expR transitions to 2^(q+1) * expR'), causing tickToPrice to be non-monotone at some tick pair (t, t+1) where tickToPrice(t) > tickToPrice(t+1), so that the binary search in priceToTick returns the wrong tick and offers are priced incorrectly? Preconditions: wExp offset property (2 * expR(-offset) == expR(ln2 - offset - 1)) must hold exactly; Attacker action: place offer at tick t where non-monotonicity occurs; Call sequence: take(offer at tick t, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: tickToPrice(t) <= tickToPrice(t+1) for all t in [0, MAX_TICK-1]; Scoped impact: binary search returns wrong tick, credit units priced incorrectly; Proof idea: exhaustive test of testTickMonotonicity() for all tick pairs, verify offset property holds in integer arithmetic.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (accumulated rounding)] Can an unprivileged lender, by executing many small takes of a buy offer (each with units=1 or small units), accumulate credit units where the total buyerAssets paid is less than the total credit units acquired due to repeated mulDivDown(buyerPrice, WAD) rounding, enabling over-redemption at maturity? Preconditions: market with buy offer at any tick < MAX_TICK; Attacker action: lender takes offer N times with units=1 each time; Call sequence: N * take(buyOffer, ..., 1, ...) -> withdraw(market, N, lender, receiver) post-maturity; Invariant tested: sum(buyerAssets_i) >= total_credit_acquired for all sequences of takes; Scoped impact: lender redeems N loan tokens having paid < N loan tokens total; Proof idea: fuzz N and tick, assert sum(mulDivDown(buyerPrice, WAD) for _ in range(N)) >= N * buyerPrice / WAD.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (sell offer rounding)] Can an unprivileged taker, by taking a sell offer (offer.buy=false) where buyerAssets = units.mulDivUp(buyerPrice, WAD) rounds up, cause the taker to pay more assets than the credit units are worth at maturity, while the maker (seller) receives sellerAssets = units.mulDivUp(sellerPrice, WAD) also rounded up, creating a scenario where the protocol collects more settlement fee than the difference between buyer and seller prices? Preconditions: market with sell offer at any tick; Attacker action: taker takes sell offer with units chosen to maximize rounding discrepancy; Call sequence: take(sellOffer, ..., units, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += buyerAssets - sellerAssets exactly equals settlementFee * units / WAD; Scoped impact: settlement fee accounting desync; Proof idea: fuzz units and tick for sell offers, assert buyerAssets - sellerAssets == expected settlement fee.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (buy vs sell rounding asymmetry)] Can an unprivileged actor exploit the asymmetric rounding in take (buy offers: mulDivDown for both buyerAssets and sellerAssets; sell offers: mulDivUp for both) to create a scenario where a buy offer and a sell offer at the same tick produce different total assets transferred for the same units, causing one side to over-redeem at maturity? Preconditions: two markets with identical parameters, one with buy offer and one with sell offer at same tick; Attacker action: taker fills both offers with same units; Call sequence: take(buyOffer, ..., units, ...) and take(sellOffer, ..., units, ...); Invariant tested: economic equivalence of buy and sell offers at same tick and same units; Scoped impact: one side acquires credit units at lower effective cost; Proof idea: differential test asserting buyerAssets_buy_offer + sellerAssets_buy_offer == buyerAssets_sell_offer + sellerAssets_sell_offer for same tick and units.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (continuousFee at TTM=0)] Can an unprivileged lender take a buy offer exactly at maturity (timeToMaturity=0) where buyerPendingFeeIncrease = buyerCreditIncrease.mulDivDown(continuousFee * 0, WAD) = 0, acquiring credit units with zero pending fee obligation, so that the full credit is redeemable at maturity without any continuous fee deduction? Preconditions: market with non-zero continuousFee, block.timestamp == market.maturity; Attacker action: lender takes buy offer at exact maturity timestamp; Call sequence: warp(maturity) -> take(buyOffer, ...) -> withdraw(market, units, lender, receiver); Invariant tested: pendingFee > 0 whenever continuousFee > 0 and credit increases; Scoped impact: lender avoids continuous fee, redeems full credit 1:1; Proof idea: assert buyerPendingFeeIncrease == 0 when timeToMaturity=0 and continuousFee>0, verify this is by design or a fee bypass.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (post-maturity sell offer)] Can an unprivileged taker, by taking a sell offer post-maturity (where sellerPrice = offerPrice, not offerPrice - settlementFee), acquire credit units at a price equal to the full offerPrice rather than offerPrice - settlementFee, while the settlement fee is still charged (buyerPrice = sellerPrice + settlementFee = offerPrice + settlementFee > 1e18), causing the take to revert or the buyer to pay more than 1 loan token per credit unit? Preconditions: market post-maturity, sell offer at tick near MAX_TICK; Attacker action: taker takes sell offer post-maturity; Call sequence: warp(maturity+1) -> take(sellOffer, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: buyerPrice <= WAD for all valid takes; Scoped impact: take reverts or buyer overpays, causing fund freeze; Proof idea: assert buyerPrice = offerPrice + settlementFee <= WAD for all post-maturity sell offers.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / settlementFee (breakpoint boundary)] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the settlement fee breakpoint boundary condition (e.g., timeToMaturity == 1 days exactly) where the fee transitions between two breakpoint intervals, causing a discontinuity or incorrect fee computation that understates the settlement fee, allowing credit units to be acquired below true economic cost? Preconditions: market with different fee values at adjacent breakpoints (e.g., settlementFeeCbp1 != settlementFeeCbp2); Attacker action: lender takes buy offer at exactly TTM=1 day, 7 days, 30 days, 90 days, 180 days; Call sequence: warp(maturity - breakpoint) -> take(buyOffer, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: settlementFee is continuous at breakpoint boundaries; Scoped impact: lender pays fewer assets per credit unit at breakpoint; Proof idea: assert settlementFee(id, breakpoint) == settlementFee(id, breakpoint+1) for each breakpoint value.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (tick spacing reduction)] Can an unprivileged lender, after a tick spacing reduction (setMarketTickSpacing), place a buy offer at a newly accessible tick whose tickToPrice is lower than any previously accessible tick price, acquiring credit units at a price not previously available and enabling over-redemption at maturity? Preconditions: market with DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=4, tickSpacingSetter reduces to spacing=1; Attacker action: lender places buy offer at tick 1, 2, or 3 (previously inaccessible); Call sequence: setMarketTickSpacing(id, 1) -> take(buyOffer at tick 1, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: tick spacing reduction does not create new price points below the minimum previously accessible price; Scoped impact: lender acquires credit units at price not previously available; Proof idea: assert min(tickToPrice(t) for t in range(0, MAX_TICK, 1)) == min(tickToPrice(t) for t in range(0, MAX_TICK, 4)).

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: wExp / tickToPrice (expR sign assumption)] Can an unprivileged actor find an input x to wExp where the comment's assumption 'expR is positive because |r| < ln2 < 1e18 and |secondTerm| > |thirdTerm|' fails due to integer arithmetic truncation, causing expR to be 0 or negative, and after uint256 cast producing a large value that makes tickToPrice return an incorrect price? Preconditions: wExp called with x in valid range for tickToPrice; Attacker action: place offer at tick where expR assumption fails; Call sequence: take(offer, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: expR > 0 for all r in [-offset, ln2-offset); Scoped impact: tickToPrice returns incorrect price, credit units mispriced; Proof idea: exhaustively verify expR > 0 for all r values reachable from valid tick inputs using integer arithmetic.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (units=0 no-op)] Can an unprivileged actor call take with units=0 to trigger the maker callback (per Midnight.sol comment line 93: 'Midnight can call the callback of offers through a no-op take, even if those offers have consumed==max') without any credit/debt change, and use the callback to manipulate state (e.g., repay debt, withdraw credit) in a way that desynchronizes the credit/debt accounting from the tick price? Preconditions: offer with callback set, consumed==max; Attacker action: taker calls take with units=0; Call sequence: take(offer, ..., 0, ...) -> callback manipulates position -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: no-op take (units=0) does not change credit, debt, or withdrawable; Scoped impact: accounting desync enabling over-redemption; Proof idea: assert position state unchanged after take with units=0.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (maxAssets consumption tracking)] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the maxAssets-based consumption tracking (line 368: consumed += offer.buy ? buyerAssets : sellerAssets) where buyerAssets is computed with mulDivDown rounding, to fill a buy offer for more total units than the maker intended (since each fill consumes fewer assets than the true price due to rounding, leaving more capacity in the offer)? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets set; Attacker action: lender takes offer in many small increments; Call sequence: N * take(buyOffer with maxAssets, ..., small_units, ...) -> withdraw post-maturity; Invariant tested: total units acquired <= maxAssets / tickToPrice(tick) * WAD; Scoped impact: lender acquires more credit units than maker intended to sell; Proof idea: fuzz N and units_per_take, assert sum(units_i) <= maxAssets * WAD / tickToPrice(tick).

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / take (maxUnits vs maxAssets equivalence)] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the difference between maxUnits-based and maxAssets-based offer caps to acquire more credit units from a maxAssets offer than from an equivalent maxUnits offer at the same tick, due to mulDivDown rounding in buyerAssets computation? Preconditions: two identical buy offers, one with maxAssets=X and one with maxUnits=X*WAD/tickToPrice(tick); Attacker action: lender fills both offers to capacity; Call sequence: take(maxAssetsOffer, ...) and take(maxUnitsOffer, ...); Invariant tested: maxAssets offer and equivalent maxUnits offer yield same total units; Scoped impact: lender acquires more credit units from maxAssets offer; Proof idea: differential test asserting total units from maxAssets offer <= maxAssets * WAD / tickToPrice(tick).

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: tickToPrice / withdraw (1:1 redemption invariant)] Can an unprivileged lender demonstrate that the protocol's 1:1 credit-to-loan-token redemption in withdraw() (line 499: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(market.loanToken, receiver, units)) is not backed by sufficient loan token deposits when credit units were acquired at a tick price below 1e18, because the protocol relies on borrowers repaying debt to fund withdrawals, and tick price approximation errors cause a systematic shortfall? Preconditions: market with borrowers who repay exactly their debt units; Attacker action: lender acquires credit units at tick price p < 1e18 and redeems at maturity; Call sequence: take(buyOffer at tick t, ...) -> repay(market, debt, borrower, ...) -> withdraw(market, credit, lender, receiver); Invariant tested: withdrawable >= sum(credit redeemed) after all debt repaid; Scoped impact: protocol insolvency if tick price errors cause systematic underpayment; Proof idea: invariant test asserting withdrawable + outstanding_debt >= totalUnits at all times.
